Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a serious fungal disease that causes dark, olive-green to black lesions on leaves, fruits, and sometimes on young twigs. The fungus overwinters in fallen infected leaves on the ground.
symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown velvety spots on leaves
Scabby, rough, cracked lesions on fruit surface
Premature leaf drop and defoliation
Distorted and stunted fruit growth
Yellowing of leaves around infected areas
causes: Fungal spores spread by wind and rain splash
Cool, wet spring weather favors infection
Overwintering fungus in fallen leaves
High humidity and prolonged leaf wetness

chemical: Apply captan fungicide at green tip stage and repeat every 7-10 days
Use myclobutanil (Rally) during primary scab season
Apply mancozeb as a protective fungicide before rain events
Use dodine (Syllit) for early-season applications
Apply sulfur-based fungicides during dry periods
organic: Apply neem oil spray every 7-14 days during growing season
Use sulfur-based organic fungicides as preventive treatment
Spray copper-based fungicides (Bordeaux mixture) in early spring
Apply potassium bicarbonate solution to affected areas
Use compost tea sprays to boost plant immunity
cultural: Rake and destroy fallen infected leaves in autumn
Prune trees to improve air circulation within canopy
Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (Liberty, Enterprise, Freedom)
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Apply urea spray (5%) to fallen leaves in autumn to accelerate decomposition
Maintain proper tree spacing for adequate airflow
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides
Use Trichoderma harzianum as soil drench and foliar spray
Encourage beneficial microorganisms through compost application

prevention_tips: Choose resistant varieties when planting new trees
Monitor weather conditions and apply fungicides before rain
Start fungicide program at green tip stage
Maintain good orchard sanitation year-round

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease affecting leaves, fruit, and bark of apple trees. It causes frogeye leaf spots, fruit rot, and cankers on limbs, potentially leading to significant crop losses.
symptoms: Frogeye leaf spot - circular brown lesions with purple margins
Large, expanding brown to black rot on fruit starting from calyx end
Concentric rings of brown and black on rotting fruit
Mummified fruit remaining on tree through winter
Sunken cankers on bark of branches and trunk
causes: Fungus enters through wounds, insect damage, or natural openings
Warm, humid weather conditions promote infection
Mummified fruit and dead wood serve as inoculum sources
Stressed trees are more susceptible

chemical: Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicides during growing season
Use strobilurin fungicides (pyraclostrobin) at petal fall
Apply myclobutanil for combined scab and black rot control
Spray copper hydroxide during dormant season
organic: Apply neem oil spray as preventive measure
Use copper-based organic fungicides in early spring
Spray Bordeaux mixture during dormant season
Apply sulfur-based fungicides during growing season
cultural: Remove and destroy all mummified fruit from trees and ground
Prune out dead wood and cankers during dormant season
Remove fire-blight-killed tissue which can harbor the fungus
Maintain overall tree health through proper fertilization
Improve air circulation through proper pruning
Control insect pests to reduce wound entry points
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based products as preventive sprays
Use Trichoderma-based biofungicides on pruning wounds

prevention_tips: Remove all sources of inoculum (mummified fruit, dead wood)
Keep trees healthy and vigorous through proper care
Start fungicide applications at petal fall
Combine with scab management program for best results

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is a fungal disease that requires two hosts to complete its life cycle - apple trees and eastern red cedar (juniper). It causes distinctive orange-yellow spots on apple leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots on upper leaf surface
Small black dots (pycnia) in center of yellow spots
Tube-like projections (aecia) on undersides of leaves
Similar spots on fruit causing deformation
Premature defoliation in severe cases
causes: Requires both apple and cedar/juniper hosts nearby
Spores travel by wind from cedar galls to apple trees
Warm spring rains trigger spore release from cedar galls
Wet conditions during spring favor infection

chemical: Apply myclobutanil (Rally) at pink bud stage through petal fall
Use propiconazole (Tilt) fungicide during spring infection period
Apply triadimefon at first sign of orange spots
Spray mancozeb as protective fungicide every 7-10 days in spring
organic: Apply sulfur-based fungicides starting at pink bud stage
Use neem oil spray every 7 days during high-risk period
Apply copper-based fungicides in early spring
Spray potassium bicarbonate solution for mild infections
cultural: Remove nearby cedar and juniper trees within 1-2 miles if possible
Remove cedar galls from juniper trees before spring
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties (Redfree, Liberty, Freedom)
Improve air circulation around trees through proper spacing
Avoid planting apple trees near cedar windbreaks
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides as preventive sprays
Use beneficial microbial sprays to boost plant immune response

prevention_tips: Remove cedar/juniper trees within 2-mile radius if feasible
Plant resistant varieties in areas prone to cedar apple rust
Monitor cedar trees for galls and remove them before spring
Begin fungicide program at pink bud stage

Apple___healthy.description: The apple plant is healthy with no signs of disease. Leaves appear green, uniform in color, and free from spots, lesions, or discoloration.

maintenance: Continue regular watering schedule - deep watering 1-2 times per week
Apply balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) in early spring before bud break
Prune annually during dormant season to maintain shape and airflow
Apply dormant oil spray in late winter to control overwintering pests
Mulch around base with 2-4 inches of organic mulch
Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pest damage
Test soil pH annually - apple trees prefer pH 6.0-7.0
Thin fruit in early summer for better size and quality

prevention_tips: Maintain good orchard hygiene by removing fallen leaves and fruit
Follow an integrated pest management (IPM) program
Ensure adequate spacing between trees for air circulation
Keep trees well-watered during dry periods
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high-risk weather

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Leaves are dark green, intact, and showing normal growth patterns.

maintenance: Maintain soil pH between 4.5-5.5 (acidic) for optimal growth
Water consistently - blueberries need 1-2 inches of water per week
Apply acidic fertilizer (ammonium sulfate) in early spring
Mulch with pine needles, sawdust, or wood chips (4-6 inches deep)
Prune annually to remove old, unproductive canes
Net plants to protect fruit from birds
Test soil annually and amend with sulfur if pH is too high
Apply compost annually to improve soil organic matter

prevention_tips: Ensure proper drainage - blueberries don't tolerate waterlogging
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce disease risk
Plant in full sun for best fruit production
Space plants adequately for good air circulation
Remove and destroy any diseased plant material promptly

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry trees appears as white, powdery fungal growth on leaves, shoots, and sometimes fruit. It thrives in warm, dry conditions with high humidity.
symptoms: White powdery coating on leaf surfaces
Curling and distortion of young leaves
Stunted new shoot growth
Russeting and blemishes on fruit surface
Premature leaf drop in severe infections
causes: Fungal spores spread by wind
Warm days and cool nights with high humidity
Overcrowded canopy with poor air circulation
Excessive nitrogen fertilization promoting succulent growth

chemical: Apply myclobutanil at first sign of disease and repeat every 10-14 days
Use trifloxystrobin (Flint) fungicide during growing season
Spray propiconazole for active infections
Apply sulfur fungicide as preventive treatment in dry conditions
organic: Spray milk solution (40% milk to 60% water) weekly
Apply potassium bicarbonate (1 tablespoon per gallon of water)
Use neem oil spray every 7-14 days
Apply sulfur-based organic fungicides (avoid in temperatures above 85°F)
Spray diluted apple cider vinegar solution (3 tablespoons per gallon)
cultural: Prune to open canopy and improve air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy infected shoots and leaves
Water at base of tree to keep foliage dry
Plant in full sun locations with good air movement
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides
Use Streptomyces lydicus products as preventive treatment
Apply beneficial fungal antagonists like Ampelomyces quisqualis

prevention_tips: Maintain open canopy through proper pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation
Monitor humidity levels and apply fungicides preventively
Select powdery mildew resistant varieties when possible

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry plant is healthy with no signs of disease. Leaves are green, properly shaped, and free from powdery coatings, spots, or discoloration.

maintenance: Water deeply once a week during dry periods
Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring
Prune during dormant season to maintain shape and airflow
Apply dormant spray in late winter for pest prevention
Mulch around base to retain moisture and suppress weeds
Monitor for signs of disease and pest activity regularly
Protect fruit from bird damage with netting
Ensure proper drainage to prevent root rot

prevention_tips: Maintain clean orchard floor
Ensure adequate spacing between trees
Follow regular pruning schedule
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high-risk periods

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.disease_name: Cercospora Leaf Spot / Gray Leaf Spot
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is one of the most significant yield-limiting diseases of corn worldwide. The fungus produces rectangular, grayish-tan lesions that run parallel to leaf veins.
symptoms: Small, rectangular lesions running parallel to leaf veins
Lesions are grayish-tan to brown in color
Lesions may coalesce causing large areas of dead leaf tissue
Lower leaves are affected first, progressing upward
Severe cases cause premature death of leaves
causes: Fungus survives in infected corn residue on soil surface
Continuous corn cropping increases disease pressure
Warm temperatures (75-85°F) with high humidity and prolonged dew
No-till or minimum tillage preserves infected residue

chemical: Apply strobilurin fungicides (azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin) at VT-R1 stage
Use triazole fungicides (propiconazole) for active infections
Apply combination products (azoxystrobin + propiconazole) for broader protection
Time fungicide applications based on disease forecasting models
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides for organic production
Use neem oil spray as mild fungicide
Apply compost tea to boost plant immune response
Use biofungicides containing Bacillus species
cultural: Rotate crops - avoid continuous corn planting
Plant resistant or tolerant corn hybrids
Tillage to bury infected crop residue
Reduce plant population in fields with history of GLS
Ensure balanced fertility especially adequate potassium
Avoid late planting which increases disease risk
biological: Apply Trichoderma-based products to break down crop residue
Use Bacillus subtilis foliar applications

prevention_tips: Select resistant hybrids for areas with GLS history
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Scout fields regularly starting at V8 growth stage
Consider tillage to reduce surface residue inoculum

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust of corn is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. It produces small, circular to elongated pustules on both leaf surfaces that are cinnamon-brown to dark reddish-brown in color.
symptoms: Small, circular to elongated cinnamon-brown pustules on leaves
Pustules appear on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
Pustules break through the leaf epidermis releasing powdery spores
Chlorotic halos may surround pustules
Severe infections cause premature leaf death
causes: Spores are wind-blown from southern regions in spring
Cool to moderate temperatures (60-77°F) with high humidity
Heavy dew and frequent rainfall promote infection
Susceptible hybrids planted in favorable conditions

chemical: Apply triazole fungicides (propiconazole, tebuconazole) at first sign
Use strobilurin fungicides (azoxystrobin) for preventive control
Apply mancozeb as protective fungicide before infection
Combination fungicides provide broader spectrum control
organic: Apply sulfur-based fungicides for organic production
Use neem oil spray every 7-10 days
Apply copper-based organic fungicides
Spray baking soda solution (1 tablespoon per gallon with soap)
cultural: Plant rust-resistant corn hybrids
Plant early to avoid peak rust infection period
Ensure adequate plant nutrition and potassium levels
Remove volunteer corn plants that can harbor the fungus
Maintain proper plant spacing
biological: Apply Bacillus-based biofungicides
Use beneficial microbial products to boost plant resistance

prevention_tips: Select resistant hybrids with Rp genes
Monitor fields regularly during warm, humid conditions
Consider fungicide application if pustules appear before tasseling
Early planting reduces exposure to late-season spore loads

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern corn leaf blight (NCLB) is a foliar disease that produces large, cigar-shaped grayish-green to tan lesions on corn leaves. Severe infections can significantly reduce yields.
symptoms: Large, cigar-shaped lesions 1-6 inches long on leaves
Lesions are grayish-green to tan colored
Lesions start on lower leaves and progress upward
Dark gray spores visible on lesion surface during humid conditions
Severe cases cause entire leaves to die
causes: Fungus overwinters in infected corn debris on soil surface
Moderate temperatures (65-80°F) with heavy dews and frequent rains
Continuous corn with no-till practices increase inoculum
Susceptible hybrids in favorable weather conditions

chemical: Apply strobilurin fungicides at VT-R1 growth stage for best results
Use triazole fungicides (propiconazole) for active infections
Apply combination fungicides (triazole + strobilurin) for extended control
Timing is critical - apply before disease reaches ear leaf
organic: Apply copper hydroxide-based fungicides
Use neem oil as a mild organic fungicide
Spray compost tea to enhance plant resistance
Apply potassium bicarbonate solution
cultural: Plant resistant hybrids with Ht genes
Practice crop rotation with soybeans or other non-host crops
Tillage to incorporate infected residue and reduce inoculum
Ensure balanced soil fertility
Avoid late planting of susceptible hybrids
Maintain adequate plant populations
biological: Apply Trichoderma products to accelerate residue decomposition
Use Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides

prevention_tips: Select hybrids with both qualitative and quantitative NCLB resistance
Rotate with non-host crops to reduce residue-borne inoculum
Scout fields from V8 through grain fill
Apply fungicides when lesions appear on third leaf below ear or higher

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Leaves are green, uniform, and free from lesions, spots, or abnormal discoloration.

maintenance: Ensure consistent moisture - corn needs 1 inch of water per week
Apply nitrogen fertilizer in split applications for optimal uptake
Maintain soil pH between 6.0-6.8 for best nutrient availability
Scout fields regularly for early detection of pests and diseases
Control weeds through cultivation or approved herbicides
Ensure adequate potassium and phosphorus levels
Monitor for insect pests like corn borers and rootworms
Practice crop rotation to maintain soil health

prevention_tips: Rotate crops annually to break disease cycles
Select disease-resistant hybrids suited for your region
Maintain balanced soil nutrition through soil testing
Control insect pests that can vector diseases

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a serious fungal disease of grapes that can destroy up to 80% of the crop. It affects leaves, shoots, and especially fruit, causing hard, black, mummified berries.
symptoms: Circular reddish-brown leaf spots with dark borders
Tiny black pycnidia (fruiting bodies) within leaf spots
Fruit develops light brown soft rot that progresses rapidly
Infected berries shrivel into hard, black mummified fruit
Dark, elongated cankers on shoots and tendrils
causes: Fungus overwinters in mummified berries and infected canes
Warm, wet weather in spring triggers spore release
Rain splash disperses spores to healthy tissue
Young, rapidly growing tissues are most susceptible

chemical: Apply myclobutanil (Rally) starting at 6-inch shoot growth through 4 weeks after bloom
Use mancozeb as protective fungicide before infection periods
Apply captan plus myclobutanil for enhanced protection
Spray tebuconazole for active infections during fruit development
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides (Bordeaux mixture) before bloom
Use sulfur sprays during growing season (avoid in hot weather)
Apply neem oil as supplemental treatment
Use potassium bicarbonate spray for mild infections
cultural: Remove and destroy all mummified berries and infected canes
Prune vines to improve air circulation and sunlight penetration
Remove wild grapes near vineyard that serve as inoculum source
Train vines for good exposure and quick drying after rain
Cultivate under vines to bury mummified fruit
Harvest promptly and remove all crop residue
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides before bloom
Use Trichoderma products to break down infected debris

prevention_tips: Sanitation is the most important control measure
Remove ALL mummified fruit in winter
Begin fungicide program at early shoot growth
Most critical spray period is bloom through 4 weeks after bloom

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).scientific_name: Multiple fungi (Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, Phaeoacremonium spp., Fomitiporia spp.)
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca is a complex grapevine trunk disease caused by multiple fungal species. It causes a distinctive tiger-stripe pattern on leaves and dark spots on berries. It can lead to sudden vine death.
symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern on leaves - interveinal chlorosis with necrotic margins
Dark spots or measles-like markings on fruit
Cross-section of trunk shows dark, discolored wood
Sudden wilting and death of entire vine (apoplexy) in summer
White, soft, spongy wood rot in trunk interior
causes: Complex of fungal pathogens entering through pruning wounds
Fungi colonize the woody tissue over several years
Hot, dry weather can trigger sudden apoplexy symptoms
Stressed vines are more susceptible

chemical: No fully effective chemical treatment exists currently
Apply thiophanate-methyl to pruning wounds immediately after pruning
Sodium arsenite was historically used but is now banned in most countries
Apply triazole fungicides as wound protectants after pruning
Trunk injection with fungicides is being researched
organic: Apply paste of Trichoderma spores to pruning wounds
Use garlic extract spray as mild fungicide
Apply bioprotectant products to pruning wounds
cultural: Prune during dry weather to minimize wound infection
Make clean pruning cuts and protect large wounds with pruning paint
Delay pruning until late in dormant season when wounds heal faster
Remove severely infected vines and replace with new plants
Double pruning technique - rough cut early, final cut late
Avoid large pruning wounds - use spur pruning where possible
Maintain vine vigor through proper irrigation and nutrition
biological: Apply Trichoderma atroviride to pruning wounds within 24 hours
Use Trichoderma-based biofungicides as preventive treatment
Apply beneficial bacteria to wounds to compete with pathogens

prevention_tips: Protect all pruning wounds with wound sealant or Trichoderma
Prune in dry conditions and late in the dormant season
Ensure clean pruning tools - disinfect between vines
This is a slow-developing disease - prevention is key
Remove and burn severely affected vines to reduce inoculum

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Isariopsis leaf spot causes angular, dark brown lesions on grape leaves, primarily affecting lower and older leaves. In severe cases, it leads to defoliation and reduced vine vigor.
symptoms: Small angular dark brown spots on lower leaves
Lesions may have yellow halos
Dark fungal growth visible on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Premature leaf drop starting from lower canopy
Reduced vine vigor and fruit quality
causes: Fungus survives on infected fallen leaves and debris
Warm, humid conditions with frequent rainfall promote disease
Poor air circulation in dense canopy
Overhead irrigation increases leaf wetness duration

chemical: Apply mancozeb fungicide as preventive spray every 10-14 days
Use copper oxychloride sprays during growing season
Apply carbendazim for systemic control of active infections
Spray chlorothalonil as protective fungicide
organic: Apply Bordeaux mixture (copper sulfate + lime) as preventive spray
Use neem oil spray every 7-10 days
Apply sulfur-based fungicides in dry weather
Spray baking soda solution as mild fungicide
cultural: Prune vines to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
Remove and destroy infected fallen leaves
Train vines vertically for better light penetration
Avoid overhead irrigation - use drip irrigation instead
Ensure proper vine spacing for adequate airflow
Apply balanced fertilization avoiding excess nitrogen
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides
Use Trichoderma harzianum as soil drench and foliar spray

prevention_tips: Start preventive fungicide sprays before symptoms appear
Maintain open canopy through regular pruning
Remove all fallen leaves and debris in autumn
Monitor lower canopy for early signs of disease

Grape___healthy.description: The grapevine is healthy with no visible disease symptoms. Leaves are green, well-formed, and free from spots, lesions, or discoloration.

maintenance: Prune annually during dormant season for optimal structure
Water deeply but infrequently - 1 inch per week during growing season
Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring based on soil test
Train vines to trellis system for best air circulation and sun exposure
Monitor regularly for pests (grape berry moth, Japanese beetles)
Apply dormant spray in late winter for pest and disease prevention
Harvest promptly at maturity for best quality
Clean up fallen fruit and leaves to reduce disease risk

prevention_tips: Maintain open canopy through proper pruning and training
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering
Apply preventive fungicide program in humid climates
Scout regularly for early disease detection

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Huanglongbing (HLB), also known as citrus greening, is the most devastating disease of citrus worldwide. It is caused by a bacterium spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. There is no cure, and infected trees decline over several years.
symptoms: Asymmetric blotchy mottling on leaves (not uniform yellowing)
Yellow shoots appearing on one section of the tree
Small, lopsided, and bitter-tasting fruit
Fruit remains green at stylar end even when ripe (color inversion)
Aborted seeds in fruit - dark and shriveled
Overall tree decline with twig dieback
Zinc and manganese deficiency-like symptoms
causes: Bacteria spread primarily by Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri)
Can also spread through grafting with infected budwood
Psyllids acquire bacteria from infected trees and transmit to healthy ones
Bacteria colonize phloem tissue disrupting nutrient transport

chemical: Apply systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam) to control psyllids
Spray foliar insecticides (fenpropathrin, carbaryl) for adult psyllids
Apply horticultural oil to suppress psyllid nymphs
Use trunk injection of oxytetracycline antibiotics (where permitted)
Apply micronutrient sprays (zinc, manganese) to manage deficiency symptoms
organic: Apply kaolin clay particle film to deter psyllid feeding
Use neem oil for psyllid suppression
Apply spinosad-based insecticides for psyllid control
Spray horticultural oils to smother psyllid eggs and nymphs
cultural: Remove and destroy confirmed HLB-infected trees immediately
Plant only certified disease-free nursery stock
Coordinate area-wide psyllid management with neighboring groves
Install insect-exclusion screenhouses for young tree production
Apply enhanced nutritional programs to extend productive life of mildly infected trees
Replace removed trees with HLB-tolerant rootstock/scion combinations
Use reflective mulch to deter psyllids from landing
biological: Release Tamarixia radiata parasitic wasp for biological control of psyllids
Encourage natural predators (ladybugs, lacewings) of psyllids
Apply entomopathogenic fungi (Hirsutella citriformis) against psyllids

prevention_tips: THERE IS NO CURE - prevention and psyllid control are essential
Use only certified disease-free planting material
Implement aggressive psyllid control program
Scout regularly and remove infected trees immediately
Coordinate with regional HLB management programs
This is a regulated disease - report suspected cases to authorities

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is a major disease of peach and other stone fruits. It causes leaf spots, fruit spots, and twig cankers, and is particularly difficult to manage in warm, wet climates.
symptoms: Small, angular, dark water-soaked spots on leaves
Leaf spots turn purple-brown, centers may fall out creating shot-hole appearance
Severe defoliation in susceptible varieties
Dark, sunken, pitted spots on fruit surface
Fruit cracks at lesion sites allowing secondary infections
Small cankers on current-season twigs
causes: Bacteria survive in twig cankers and spread by rain splash
Warm temperatures (75-86°F) with wind-driven rain favor infection
Sandy soils and nutritional stress increase susceptibility
Bacteria enter through stomata and wounds

chemical: Apply copper hydroxide sprays at leaf fall and before bud break
Use oxytetracycline (Mycoshield) during growing season
Apply copper + mancozeb tank mix for enhanced efficacy
Avoid copper after shuck split to prevent fruit russeting
organic: Apply fixed copper fungicides during dormant season
Use copper sulfate (Bordeaux mixture) at leaf fall
Apply Bacillus subtilis-based products during growing season
Spray copper at reduced rates to minimize phytotoxicity
cultural: Plant resistant varieties (Contender, Harrow Beauty, Redhaven)
Avoid planting in low-lying areas with poor air circulation
Ensure adequate nutrition especially nitrogen and zinc
Prune to open canopy for faster drying of foliage
Use windbreaks to reduce wind-driven rain spread
Remove severely infected trees that serve as inoculum source
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis products as part of spray program
Use bacteriophage-based products where available

prevention_tips: Variety selection is the most effective management tool
Begin copper sprays at leaf fall to reduce overwintering inoculum
Maintain adequate tree nutrition especially on sandy soils
This is a very difficult disease to control - integrated approach is essential

Peach___healthy.description: The peach tree is healthy with no visible disease symptoms. Leaves are green, properly shaped, and free from spots or discoloration.

maintenance: Prune annually in late winter before bud break
Apply balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) in spring after bloom
Water deeply during fruit development - 1-2 inches per week
Thin fruit to 6-8 inches apart for best size and quality
Apply dormant spray of copper + horticultural oil in late winter
Monitor for peach tree borers and oriental fruit moth
Mulch around base but keep mulch away from trunk
Maintain soil pH between 6.0-6.5

prevention_tips: Follow proper pruning practices to maintain open center
Apply preventive fungicide program for brown rot and leaf curl
Monitor for bacterial spot especially in wet years
Control insect pests to prevent wound entry for diseases

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is one of the most devastating diseases of pepper and tomato. It causes leaf spots, fruit spots, and defoliation, leading to sunscalded fruit and significant yield losses.
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked circular spots on leaves
Leaf spots become angular, brown with yellow halos
Severe defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
Raised, scab-like spots on fruit surface
Spots on stems and petioles
Wilting of young transplants in severe cases
causes: Bacteria spread by rain splash, overhead irrigation, and contaminated tools
Infected seed is a primary source of inoculum
Warm temperatures (75-86°F) with high humidity
Workers handling wet plants spread bacteria

chemical: Apply copper hydroxide + mancozeb tank mix weekly during wet weather
Use copper-based bactericides starting at transplanting
Apply acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) as systemic resistance inducer
Alternate copper products with biological controls
organic: Apply fixed copper sprays every 7-10 days during wet weather
Use copper sulfate at reduced rates to minimize phytotoxicity
Apply Bacillus subtilis products between copper sprays
Spray hydrogen peroxide-based sanitizers
cultural: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Treat seed with hot water (125°F for 25 minutes) before planting
Practice crop rotation - avoid peppers and tomatoes for 2-3 years
Avoid overhead irrigation - use drip irrigation
Do not work in fields when plants are wet
Remove and destroy severely infected plants
Sanitize all tools and equipment between fields
Plant resistant varieties when available
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based products (Serenade)
Use bacteriophage-based products where available
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens as competitive colonizer

prevention_tips: Start with clean seed and transplants - this is critical
Begin copper sprays at transplanting before symptoms appear
Avoid overhead irrigation and working in wet fields
Rotate away from solanaceous crops for at least 2 years

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The bell pepper plant is healthy with no visible disease symptoms. Leaves are dark green, firm, and free from spots or wilting.

maintenance: Water consistently - peppers need 1-2 inches per week
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Apply balanced fertilizer at planting and side-dress at flowering
Mulch with straw or plastic to retain moisture and reduce splash
Stake or cage plants to keep fruit off the ground
Monitor for aphids, pepper weevils, and hornworms
Harvest fruit regularly to encourage continued production
Maintain soil pH between 6.0-6.8

prevention_tips: Rotate crops - don't plant peppers after tomatoes or potatoes
Use disease-resistant varieties when available
Keep foliage dry through proper irrigation practices
Remove plant debris at end of season

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is a common fungal disease of potato that causes characteristic concentric-ringed (target-shaped) lesions on leaves. It typically appears during warm, humid weather and can significantly reduce yields.
symptoms: Dark brown to black lesions with concentric rings (target/bullseye pattern)
Lesions first appear on older, lower leaves
Yellow halo around lesions
Progressive defoliation from bottom of plant upward
Dark, sunken, circular lesions on tubers
Collar rot on stems near soil line
causes: Fungus overwinters in infected plant debris and soil
Warm temperatures (75-84°F) with alternating wet and dry periods
Stressed plants (nutrient deficiency, drought) are more susceptible
Spores spread by wind, rain splash, and irrigation water

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil (Bravo) as protective fungicide every 7-10 days
Use azoxystrobin (Quadris) for systemic control
Apply mancozeb as economical protective fungicide
Use difenoconazole + mandipropamid for advanced infections
Rotate fungicide modes of action to prevent resistance
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7-10 days
Use Bacillus subtilis products (Serenade) as biofungicide
Spray neem oil every 7 days as preventive treatment
Apply compost tea to boost plant immune system
cultural: Practice 3-year crop rotation away from solanaceous crops
Remove and destroy infected plant debris after harvest
Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes
Hill potatoes to prevent tuber infection from spore wash
Ensure adequate and balanced fertilization
Irrigate in morning so foliage dries quickly
Plant resistant or tolerant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation
biological: Apply Trichoderma harzianum as soil treatment
Use Bacillus amyloliquefaciens-based products
Apply beneficial fungi to compete with pathogen in soil

prevention_tips: Start fungicide program before symptoms appear in high-risk conditions
Maintain adequate plant nutrition - stressed plants are most susceptible
Destroy all plant debris and volunteer potatoes
Use a combination of resistant varieties and fungicide program

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is the most destructive disease of potato, historically responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It can destroy entire fields within days under favorable conditions and requires immediate, aggressive management.
symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked lesions on leaves, often starting at leaf tips
White, fuzzy fungal growth on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Lesions turn dark brown to black rapidly
Entire plant can collapse and die within days
Reddish-brown, granular rot extending into potato tubers
Foul smell from decaying plant tissue
causes: Oomycete pathogen that thrives in cool (50-70°F), wet conditions
Sporangia spread rapidly by wind and rain over long distances
Infected seed potatoes and volunteer plants serve as inoculum
Cull piles of infected tubers are major source of spores

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil as protective fungicide before infection occurs
Use mefenoxam/metalaxyl + mancozeb for systemic + protective control
Apply cymoxanil + mancozeb for curative action within 2 days of infection
Use fluopicolide or mandipropamid for resistant strains
Apply fungicides every 5-7 days during high-risk weather
Spray immediately if late blight is reported in your region
organic: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate every 5-7 days
Use Bacillus subtilis products between copper sprays
Apply copper at higher rates than for early blight
Potassium bicarbonate as supplemental spray
cultural: Plant only certified disease-free seed potatoes
Destroy all cull piles and volunteer potatoes
Plant resistant varieties (Defender, Elba, Kennebec)
Destroy entire crop immediately if late blight is confirmed
Kill vines 2-3 weeks before harvest to prevent tuber infection
Harvest in dry conditions and cure tubers before storage
Do NOT compost infected material - burn or deep bury it
Avoid overhead irrigation
biological: Biological control is limited for this aggressive pathogen
Trichoderma products may provide supplemental protection
Use in combination with other methods only

prevention_tips: THIS IS AN EMERGENCY DISEASE - act immediately when detected
Monitor late blight forecasting systems in your region
Begin fungicide program before any reports in your area
Destroy all sources of inoculum (cull piles, volunteers)
Report confirmed cases to your extension service

Potato___healthy.description: The potato plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Foliage is green, vigorous, and free from lesions, spots, or wilting.

maintenance: Water consistently - potatoes need 1-2 inches per week
Hill soil around stems as plants grow to protect developing tubers
Apply balanced fertilizer at planting and side-dress at tuber initiation
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle and aphids
Scout regularly for early and late blight symptoms
Maintain soil pH between 5.0-6.5
Apply mulch to keep soil cool and moist
Cure harvested tubers in dark, well-ventilated space at 50-60°F

prevention_tips: Use only certified seed potatoes
Rotate crops on a 3-year cycle
Destroy volunteer potatoes and cull piles
Monitor late blight forecasting services in your region

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Canes are vigorous, leaves are green and well-formed, and there are no signs of infection.

maintenance: Prune spent floricanes after fruiting for summer-bearing types
Water consistently - 1-1.5 inches per week during growing season
Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring
Mulch with straw or wood chips to retain moisture and control weeds
Train canes to trellis system for support and air circulation
Remove weak, diseased, or crowded canes annually
Net plants to protect fruit from birds
Test soil annually and maintain pH between 5.5-6.5

prevention_tips: Maintain good air circulation through proper spacing and pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation
Plant certified virus-free stock
Remove old canes promptly to reduce disease habitat

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Leaves are trifoliate, green, and free from spots, lesions, or abnormal discoloration.

maintenance: Ensure proper inoculation with Bradyrhizobium japonicum at planting
Water during critical reproductive stages (R1-R6) if dry
Scout for soybean aphids, bean leaf beetles, and stink bugs
Monitor for sudden death syndrome and white mold
Maintain soil fertility based on soil test recommendations
Control weeds through cultivation or herbicide program
Plant at appropriate row spacing and population for your region
Harvest promptly at maturity to minimize field losses

prevention_tips: Rotate with corn or other non-legume crops
Select varieties with disease resistance packages suited to your area
Avoid planting in poorly drained fields
Use certified treated seed for best stand establishment

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew is the most common disease of squash and other cucurbits. It causes a white, powdery coating on leaves that reduces photosynthesis and can significantly affect yield and fruit quality.
symptoms: White, powdery talc-like coating on upper leaf surfaces
May also appear on lower leaf surfaces, stems, and petioles
Yellowing and browning of affected leaves
Premature leaf death and defoliation
Reduced fruit size, number, and quality
Sunscald on exposed fruit due to defoliation
causes: Fungal spores spread by wind - does NOT require free water
Moderate temperatures (68-80°F) with high humidity but dry leaves
Shaded conditions and crowded plantings increase susceptibility
Late-season plantings are often more severely affected

chemical: Apply myclobutanil at first sign of disease
Use chlorothalonil as protective fungicide
Apply triflumizole or triadimefon for systemic control
Alternate fungicides with different modes of action
Spray both upper and lower leaf surfaces thoroughly
organic: Apply potassium bicarbonate (1 tablespoon per gallon with liquid soap)
Spray milk solution (40% milk to 60% water) every 7 days
Apply sulfur-based fungicides (avoid when temperature exceeds 90°F)
Use neem oil spray every 7-10 days
Apply horticultural oil (1-2%) as preventive spray
Spray diluted hydrogen peroxide solution
cultural: Plant powdery mildew resistant varieties
Space plants adequately for good air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves
Plant in full sun locations
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Plant early to harvest before peak disease pressure
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) every 7-14 days
Use Streptomyces lydicus-based products
Apply Ampelomyces quisqualis - a natural parasite of powdery mildew

prevention_tips: Plant resistant varieties - this is the most effective strategy
Begin fungicide or organic spray program at first sign of disease
Monitor closely starting mid-season when disease pressure increases
Milk spray is surprisingly effective as a low-cost organic option

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch is a common fungal disease of strawberry that causes numerous small, dark purple spots on leaves. Severe infections lead to a scorched appearance and can significantly weaken plants.
symptoms: Numerous small, dark purple irregular spots on upper leaf surface
Spots may merge giving leaves a burned or scorched appearance
Affected leaves curl upward and appear dried
Dark spots on petioles, stolons, and fruit stalks
Weakened plants with reduced fruit production
Severely affected leaves die and remain attached to plant
causes: Fungus overwinters in infected dead leaves
Spores spread by rain splash and overhead irrigation
Warm temperatures (70-85°F) with frequent rainfall or irrigation
Crowded plantings with poor air circulation

chemical: Apply captan fungicide every 7-10 days during wet periods
Use myclobutanil for systemic control
Apply azoxystrobin (Quadris) as preventive treatment
Spray copper-based fungicides in early spring
organic: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate sprays
Use neem oil spray every 7-10 days
Apply Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides
Spray potassium bicarbonate solution
cultural: Plant resistant varieties (Allstar, Earliglow, Sparkle)
Renovate beds after harvest - mow foliage and narrow rows
Avoid overhead irrigation - use drip irrigation
Improve air circulation through proper plant spacing
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Rotate strawberry beds every 3-4 years
Mulch with straw to reduce rain splash
biological: Apply Trichoderma-based products to soil
Use Bacillus subtilis foliar sprays
Encourage beneficial soil microorganisms through compost

prevention_tips: Select resistant varieties for areas with disease history
Renovate beds promptly after harvest
Avoid overhead irrigation especially during fruiting
Maintain proper plant spacing and bed width

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry plant is healthy with no visible disease symptoms. Leaves are dark green, trifoliate, and free from spots or scorching.

maintenance: Water 1-1.5 inches per week using drip irrigation
Apply balanced fertilizer at planting and after first harvest
Mulch with straw to keep fruit clean and retain moisture
Remove runners regularly unless propagating new plants
Renovate June-bearing beds after harvest
Monitor for spider mites, slugs, and tarnished plant bugs
Harvest fruit promptly when ripe
Protect from frost in spring with row covers
Replace beds every 3-4 years for best production

prevention_tips: Use certified disease-free transplants
Maintain proper spacing for air circulation
Rotate planting areas every few years
Remove diseased plants promptly if symptoms appear

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.scientific_name: Xanthomonas vesicatoria / X. euvesicatoria / X. gardneri / X. perforans
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of tomato is caused by several Xanthomonas species. It causes leaf spots, fruit spots, and defoliation, and is extremely difficult to control once established, especially in warm, wet conditions.
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked circular spots on leaves
Spots turn brown with yellow halos, may have greasy appearance
Severe defoliation leading to sunscalded fruit
Small, raised, scab-like spots on green fruit
Spots on fruit become brown and rough as fruit matures
Spots on stems and petioles
causes: Bacteria spread by rain splash, wind-driven rain, and contaminated tools
Infected seed is a primary source of contamination
Warm temperatures (75-86°F) with frequent rain or overhead irrigation
Volunteer plants and crop debris harbor bacteria

chemical: Apply copper hydroxide + mancozeb tank mix weekly in wet weather
Use fixed copper products (Kocide, Badge) as bactericides
Apply acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) as systemic resistance inducer
Streptomycin may be used on transplants (check local regulations)
organic: Apply fixed copper sprays every 7-10 days
Use copper sulfate-based Bordeaux mixture
Apply Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) between copper applications
Hydrogen peroxide-based sanitizers as supplemental spray
cultural: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Hot water treat seed (122°F for 25 minutes) before planting
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation away from solanaceous crops
Use drip irrigation - AVOID overhead watering
Stake and prune plants for better air circulation
Do not work in fields when plants are wet
Sanitize tools with 10% bleach or 70% alcohol between plants
Remove and destroy severely infected plants
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis or B. amyloliquefaciens products
Use bacteriophage products where commercially available
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens as competitive colonizer

prevention_tips: Start with clean seed and transplants - this is the most critical step
Begin copper sprays at transplanting BEFORE symptoms appear
Never use overhead irrigation on tomatoes
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is one of the most common diseases of tomato. It causes distinctive target-shaped lesions on leaves, usually starting on older, lower leaves and progressing upward through the plant.
symptoms: Dark brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target/bullseye pattern)
Lesions start on lower, older leaves first
Yellow halo around lesions
Progressive defoliation from bottom of plant upward
Dark, leathery, sunken spots on fruit at stem end
Dark cankers may appear on stems
causes: Fungus overwinters in infected plant debris and soil
Warm temperatures (75-85°F) with alternating wet and dry periods
Stressed or nutrient-deficient plants are more susceptible
Spores spread by wind, rain splash, and overhead irrigation

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil (Daconil) every 7-10 days as protective spray
Use azoxystrobin (Quadris) for systemic protection
Apply mancozeb as economical protective fungicide
Use difenoconazole or penthiopyrad for advanced control
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance development
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7-10 days
Use Bacillus subtilis products (Serenade) as biofungicide
Spray neem oil every 7 days for prevention
Apply compost tea to boost plant immunity
Use potassium bicarbonate spray
cultural: Practice crop rotation - 3 year minimum away from solanaceous crops
Mulch heavily to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves
Remove lower leaves up to first fruit cluster as plants grow
Stake or cage plants to improve air circulation
Water at base of plants - avoid overhead irrigation
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Ensure adequate balanced nutrition
Plant resistant varieties when available
biological: Apply Trichoderma harzianum to soil at planting
Use Bacillus amyloliquefaciens foliar sprays
Apply compost rich in beneficial microorganisms

prevention_tips: Mulching is extremely effective at reducing early blight
Remove lower leaves as the plant grows to reduce splash infection
Start fungicide program before symptoms appear
Maintain adequate nitrogen and calcium levels

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is one of the most destructive diseases of tomato, caused by the same pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It can destroy entire plantings within days under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked grayish-green spots on leaves
White fuzzy mold growth on underside of leaves in humid weather
Lesions turn dark brown to black rapidly
Greasy-looking dark spots on stems
Firm, dark brown rot on fruit with rough, greasy appearance
Entire plants can collapse and die within days
causes: Thrives in cool (50-70°F), wet conditions
Sporangia spread rapidly by wind over long distances
Infected transplants, volunteer plants, and nearby potato fields
Prolonged periods of wet foliage and high humidity

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil as protective fungicide BEFORE infection
Use mefenoxam/metalaxyl + mancozeb for systemic protection
Apply cymoxanil for curative action (effective within 2 days of infection)
Use mandipropamid or fluopicolide for resistant strains
Spray every 5-7 days during high-risk weather conditions
organic: Apply copper hydroxide at maximum labeled rate every 5-7 days
Use Bacillus subtilis between copper applications
Apply copper sulfate (Bordeaux mixture) as heavy-duty spray
Potassium bicarbonate as supplemental treatment
cultural: Remove and DESTROY (not compost) all infected plants immediately
Do not plant near potato fields
Ensure good air circulation through proper spacing and staking
Water at base only - never overhead irrigate
Plant resistant varieties (Mountain Magic, Defiant PHR, Iron Lady)
Use row covers or high tunnels to keep foliage dry
Kill plants before composting at end of season
Monitor late blight forecasting systems
biological: Limited biological control effectiveness for this aggressive pathogen
Bacillus-based products provide supplemental protection only
Must be used in combination with other methods

prevention_tips: THIS IS AN EMERGENCY DISEASE - remove infected plants IMMEDIATELY
Monitor late blight alerts from your local extension service
Start preventive fungicides when late blight is reported in your area
NEVER compost late blight-infected plant material

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.scientific_name: Passalora fulva (formerly Fulvia fulva / Cladosporium fulvum)
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mold is a fungal disease primarily affecting greenhouse and high-tunnel tomatoes. It causes pale green to yellowish spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green to brown velvety mold on the undersides.
symptoms: Pale green to yellowish irregular spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grayish-brown velvety mold growth on leaf undersides
Infected leaves curl, wither, and drop
Progresses from lower to upper leaves
Rarely affects fruit but can cause blossom drop
Plants in enclosed structures are most severely affected
causes: High relative humidity (above 85%) with moderate temperatures
Poor air circulation in greenhouses and high tunnels
Spores persist in greenhouse structures for years
Overcrowded planting with poor ventilation

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil as protective fungicide
Use mancozeb as economical preventive spray
Apply azoxystrobin for systemic control
Use penthiopyrad or difenoconazole for active infections
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7-10 days
Use potassium bicarbonate spray
Apply neem oil spray weekly
Use sulfur-based fungicides (avoid in hot weather)
cultural: Increase ventilation in greenhouses - this is the most critical step
Use fans to improve air circulation around plants
Reduce humidity by venting and heating
Space plants adequately and prune lower leaves
Avoid leaf wetness - water at base of plants early in day
Remove infected leaves promptly
Plant leaf mold resistant varieties for greenhouse production
Sanitize greenhouse between crops
Avoid overhead irrigation
biological: Apply Bacillus subtilis-based products
Use Trichoderma-based biofungicides
Apply beneficial bacteria to create competitive environment on leaf surfaces

prevention_tips: Ventilation management is the key to preventing leaf mold
Keep relative humidity below 85% in enclosed structures
Plant resistant varieties for greenhouse/high tunnel production
Remove and destroy infected foliage promptly
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.spread_rate: Moderate to High in enclosed spaces

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is one of the most common and destructive diseases of tomato foliage. It causes many small, circular spots with dark borders and gray centers, leading to severe defoliation.
symptoms: Numerous small (1/8 inch) circular spots on leaves
Spots have dark brown borders with grayish-white centers
Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in center of spots under magnification
First appears on lower, older leaves
Severe cases cause extensive defoliation
Rarely affects fruit directly but defoliation leads to sunscald
causes: Fungus survives in infected tomato debris in soil
Spores spread by rain splash, overhead irrigation, and tools
Warm temperatures (68-77°F) with wet, humid conditions
Continuous tomato planting without rotation

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil at first sign of disease, repeat every 7-10 days
Use mancozeb as protective fungicide during wet periods
Apply azoxystrobin for systemic control
Use copper-based fungicides as alternative protective treatment
Rotate fungicide classes to prevent resistance
organic: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate every 7-10 days
Use Bacillus subtilis products (Serenade) as biofungicide
Spray neem oil every 7 days
Apply potassium bicarbonate solution weekly
cultural: Rotate crops - 3 year minimum without solanaceous crops
Mulch heavily to prevent rain splash from soil to lower leaves
Remove lower leaves up to first fruit cluster
Stake and prune plants for maximum air circulation
Use drip irrigation - never overhead water
Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately
Clean up all tomato debris at end of season
Sanitize stakes and cages between seasons
biological: Apply Trichoderma harzianum to soil to break down infected debris
Use Bacillus amyloliquefaciens foliar sprays
Apply compost to promote beneficial soil microorganisms

prevention_tips: Mulching is the single most effective cultural control
Start removing lower leaves as soon as first fruit sets
Begin fungicide sprays before symptoms appear during wet weather
Combine cultural practices with fungicide program for best results

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites are tiny arachnid pests that feed on tomato leaves by piercing cells and sucking contents. They cause stippling, yellowing, and bronzing of leaves. Severe infestations can kill plants, especially in hot, dry conditions.
symptoms: Fine yellow stippling dots on upper leaf surface
Leaves turn yellow, bronze, and eventually brown
Fine silken webbing on undersides of leaves and between stems
Tiny (0.5mm) yellowish-green mites with two dark spots on body
Mites and eggs visible on leaf undersides with hand lens
Leaf drop and plant decline in severe infestations
causes: Hot, dry, dusty conditions favor rapid reproduction
Overuse of broad-spectrum insecticides kills natural predators
Drought-stressed plants are more attractive to mites
Mites reproduce very rapidly - population doubles every few days

chemical: Apply miticide (abamectin, spiromesifen) - regular insecticides are NOT effective
Use bifenazate (Acramite) for quick knockdown of mites
Apply spiromesifen (Oberon) for long-lasting control
Rotate miticide classes to prevent resistance
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill predatory mites
organic: Spray insecticidal soap (potassium salts of fatty acids) - thorough coverage needed
Apply neem oil spray every 5-7 days, covering all leaf surfaces
Use horticultural oil (1-2%) to smother mites and eggs
Spray rosemary oil-based miticides
Apply pyrethrin sprays for quick knockdown (use sparingly)
cultural: Increase humidity around plants through misting or irrigation
Avoid water stress - keep plants well-watered
Knock mites off plants with strong water spray
Remove heavily infested leaves and destroy them
Keep area free of weeds that can harbor mites
Reduce dusty conditions near plants
Avoid excessive nitrogen which produces succulent growth mites prefer
biological: Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) - most effective biocontrol
Use Neoseiulus californicus predatory mites for prevention
Release minute pirate bugs (Orius spp.) as generalist predators
Encourage ladybugs and lacewings which also feed on mites
Apply Beauveria bassiana entomopathogenic fungus

prevention_tips: Scout regularly by checking leaf undersides with hand lens
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that eliminate natural enemies
Keep plants well-watered to reduce stress
Release predatory mites preventively in greenhouses
Treat early - mite populations explode rapidly
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.spread_rate: Very High in hot, dry conditions

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot is a fungal disease causing distinctive target-like concentric ring lesions on tomato leaves, stems, and fruit. It has become increasingly important in warm, humid tomato-growing regions.
symptoms: Small, dark brown circular spots with concentric rings on leaves
Spots may have yellowish halo
Lesions start on lower leaves and progress upward
Large irregular spots can form on fruit with dark borders
Defoliation in severe cases
Dark spots on stems
causes: Fungus survives in infected plant debris and soil
Warm temperatures (68-86°F) with high humidity and rainfall
Dense canopy with poor air circulation
Prolonged leaf wetness promotes infection

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil as protective fungicide every 7-10 days
Use azoxystrobin for systemic control
Apply boscalid + pyraclostrobin for enhanced protection
Use difenoconazole for active infections
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7-10 days
Use Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides
Spray neem oil as supplemental treatment
Apply potassium bicarbonate spray
cultural: Remove lower leaves to improve air circulation
Stake and prune plants for open canopy structure
Use drip irrigation - avoid overhead watering
Rotate crops for at least 2-3 years
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Mulch to reduce soil splash onto plants
Ensure adequate spacing between plants
biological: Apply Trichoderma-based products to soil
Use Bacillus-based foliar sprays
Encourage beneficial soil microbiome through compost application

prevention_tips: Maintain open canopy through proper pruning and staking
Start fungicide program before wet weather periods
Remove lower leaves as plants grow
Good sanitation and crop rotation are essential

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: TYLCV is one of the most devastating viral diseases of tomato worldwide. It is transmitted by whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci) and causes severe stunting, leaf curling, and yellowing, with complete crop loss in severe cases.
symptoms: Severe upward curling and cupping of leaves
Yellowing of leaf margins and interveinal areas
Stunted, bushy plant growth
Flower drop and drastically reduced fruit set
Small, pale fruit if any are produced
Plants infected early produce no marketable fruit
causes: Virus transmitted by whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci, B biotype)
Whiteflies acquire virus from infected plants and transmit to healthy ones
Virus is NOT seed-transmitted or mechanically transmitted
Warm temperatures favor whitefly reproduction and virus spread

chemical: Apply systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam) at transplanting for whitefly control
Use spiromesifen or cyantraniliprole for whitefly nymph control
Apply pyriproxyfen (insect growth regulator) to break whitefly life cycle
Alternate insecticide classes to prevent resistance
NO CHEMICAL TREATMENT EXISTS FOR THE VIRUS ITSELF
organic: Apply insecticidal soap for whitefly control
Use neem oil spray every 5-7 days to deter whiteflies
Apply horticultural oil to smother whitefly eggs and nymphs
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly populations
Spray kaolin clay particle film to deter whitefly feeding
cultural: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties (Tygress, Shanty, many modern hybrids)
Use reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Install fine-mesh insect exclusion screens on greenhouses
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately upon detection
Maintain a host-free period between tomato crops
Remove weeds that can harbor whiteflies and virus
Transplant healthy seedlings raised under insect-proof screens
Avoid planting new tomato crops adjacent to old, infected fields
biological: Release Encarsia formosa parasitic wasp for whitefly control in greenhouses
Use Eretmocerus eremicus for biological control of whiteflies
Apply Beauveria bassiana entomopathogenic fungus against whiteflies
Encourage natural predators (lacewings, ladybugs, predatory bugs)

prevention_tips: THERE IS NO CURE FOR VIRUS-INFECTED PLANTS - prevention is everything
Whitefly management is the key to preventing TYLCV
Plant resistant varieties whenever available
Use insect-proof screenhouses for seedling production
Remove and destroy infected plants as soon as symptoms appear

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus is a highly stable and contagious viral disease that causes mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves. Unlike many plant viruses, it is easily transmitted mechanically through contaminated hands, tools, and seed.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green patches on leaves
Leaf curling and distortion (fernleaf symptoms)
Stunted plant growth
Reduced fruit size and yield
Yellow mottling or brown spots on fruit (internal browning)
Plants may show symptoms intermittently depending on temperature
causes: Virus is highly stable and can persist on surfaces for years
Transmitted mechanically through contaminated hands, tools, clothing
Can be seed-borne (survives on seed coat)
Tobacco products (cigarettes) can carry related tobacco mosaic virus
NOT transmitted by insects

chemical: NO CHEMICAL TREATMENT EXISTS FOR VIRAL INFECTIONS
Disinfect tools with 20% bleach solution or 1% sodium hypochlorite
Dip hands in milk before handling plants (milk protein inactivates virus)
Use trisodium phosphate (TSP) solution to clean tools and surfaces
organic: Apply milk spray (20% milk solution) to plants - casein protein inhibits virus
No effective organic treatment for established infections
Focus on prevention through sanitation
cultural: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Wash hands thoroughly with soap before and after handling plants
Disinfect all tools, stakes, trays between use
Use certified virus-free seed or treat seed with 10% TSP for 30 minutes
Do not smoke or use tobacco products near tomato plants
Plant resistant varieties with Tm-2 or Tm-2(2) resistance genes
Start with virus-free transplants from reputable sources
Do not touch healthy plants after handling infected ones
Change clothing after working with infected plants
biological: Cross-protection using mild virus strains (commercial technique)
No practical biological control for home gardeners

prevention_tips: SANITATION IS THE MOST IMPORTANT CONTROL MEASURE
Plant resistant varieties - Tm-2 gene provides excellent resistance
Wash hands and disinfect tools constantly
This virus is extremely stable - it survives on surfaces for years
Do not save seed from infected plants

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato plant is healthy with no visible signs of disease. Leaves are dark green, properly formed, and free from spots, curling, mosaic patterns, or other abnormalities.

maintenance: Water consistently - 1-2 inches per week at base of plants
Use drip irrigation - avoid overhead watering
Mulch with straw, hay, or leaves (3-4 inches deep)
Stake or cage plants for support and air circulation
Remove suckers from indeterminate varieties for better airflow
Apply balanced fertilizer - avoid excess nitrogen
Add calcium (lime, gypsum) to prevent blossom end rot
Monitor for tomato hornworms, aphids, and whiteflies
Scout regularly for early signs of disease
Prune lower leaves as plant grows to prevent soil splash

prevention_tips: Rotate crops on a 3-4 year cycle
Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Maintain proper spacing (24-36 inches between plants)
Apply preventive fungicide program in humid climates
Remove all plant debris at end of season
Disinfect stakes and cages between seasons

Banana___Cordana.description: Cordana leaf spot is a fungal disease causing oval, pale brown necrotic spots with dark borders, primarily on older leaves.

chemical: Apply appropriate fungicides like Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil
Use protective fungicidal sprays during high humidity
organic: Spray neem oil extracts as a natural deterrent
Apply copper-based organic sprays
cultural: Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Ensure proper weed control and drainage in the plantation
Maintain adequate spacing between plants to improve airflow
biological: Utilize Bacillus subtilis-based products to suppress fungal growth

prevention_tips: Prune appropriately to reduce canopy humidity
Avoid overhead irrigation
Monitor plants regularly during wet seasons

Banana___healthy.description: The banana plant appears healthy and shows no visible signs of Cordana, Pestalotiopsis, or Sigatoka diseases.

maintenance: Continue regular watering and fertilization
Monitor for early signs of pests or diseases
Maintain clean farm practices

prevention_tips: Conduct routine inspections of older and younger leaves
Maintain optimal soil health with balanced nutrients

Banana___Pestalotiopsis.description: Pestalotiopsis is a fungal infection characterized by tip dieback and leaf spots with grayish centers and dark margins.

chemical: Use approved broad-spectrum fungicides
Apply systemic fungicides if the infection is severe
organic: Apply sulfur or copper botanical sprays based on local guidelines
cultural: Cut off and burn infected leaves to reduce the source of spores
Improve plant nutrition (especially potassium) to enhance resistance
biological: Encourage beneficial soil microorganisms through compost

prevention_tips: Avoid bruising or damaging leaves during farm operations
Maintain balanced fertilization, avoiding excess nitrogen
Clear plantation debris regularly

Banana___Sigatoka.description: Sigatoka (Yellow or Black) is a devastating foliar disease causing small yellow spots that enlarge into brown/black streaks, drastically reducing photosynthesis and yield.

chemical: Apply systemic fungicides like Propiconazole or Difenoconazole
Rotate fungicide classes to prevent resistance buildup
Use protectants like Mancozeb in combination with mineral oils
organic: Limited organic control available for severe outbreaks; rely on mineral oil sprays to slow spread
cultural: De-leaf infected areas promptly and leave them on the ground to decompose quickly or remove them
Enhance drainage and aeration around the bananas
Plant resistant or tolerant banana cultivars when possible
biological: Use bio-fungicides based on Trichoderma or Bacillus species protectively

prevention_tips: Implement a strict de-leafing and sanitation program
Utilize an early warning system based on weather data to time fungicide applications
Ensure wide plant spacing
